place: Port Washington New York, New York

facts.text: Port Washington was settled in 1644 when 18 English families bought land from the Matinecock Indians and named it “Cow Neck.”
In the 1870s, Port Washington became a sand‑mining town; its sandbank was the largest east of the Mississippi and supplied concrete for skyscrapers like the Empire State Building.
The John Philip Sousa Memorial Bandshell, dedicated in 1967, sits in Sunset Park and honors the composer who once lived nearby.
The Long Island Rail Road extended its Port Washington Branch to the hamlet in 1898, spurring rapid development and making the town a commuter hub.
Port Washington’s waterfront hosts the Harbor Links golf course, a former sand‑mine redeveloped in 1998 for North Hempstead residents.
The hamlet’s historic Thomas Dodge Homestead, Main Street School, and Monfort Cemetery are listed on the National Register of Historic Places.
